title: Sandbag Technique
text:
During an emergency, speed is important, but technique is vital. A poorly built wall will fail when the water pressure rises. Follow these steps to ensure our sanctuary stays dry.



Materials: You will need burlap or plastic sandbags, coarse sand (avoid fine “play sand” if possible), shovels, and heavy-duty gloves.
The Two-Person Team: One person holds the bag while the second person shovels.
Tip: The person holding the bag should stand with their knees slightly bent to protect their back. Fold the top of the bag over to create a “handle” for easier filling.
Don’t Overfill: Fill bags only 1/2 to 2/3 full. This allows the bag to be “squished” into the shape of the bag below it, creating a tight seal.





Never just throw bags into a pile. They must be laid with precision.

Clear the Area: Remove all debris, ice, or large rocks from the area where the wall will sit.
The Staggered Fold: Place the first bag with the “tucked” or tied end pointing upstream (the direction the water is coming from).
The “Brick” Pattern: Lay bags like bricks in a wall. The middle of a bag in the second layer should sit directly over the “seam” where two bags meet in the first layer.
Tamp Down: After laying each bag, step on it or hit it with a shovel to flatten it. This removes air pockets and forces the sand to fill the gaps.





If the water is expected to be more than one foot high, you cannot build a vertical wall; it will topple. You must build a Pyramid.

Rule of Thumb: The base of your wall should be three times wider than the wall is high (e.g., If you need a 1-foot high wall, the base should be 3 feet wide).
Caulking the Seams: As you build upward, continue tamping the bags to ensure the layers “lock” together.





Sandbags are porous—water will eventually seep through the fabric. To create a true barrier, use 6-mil plastic sheeting.

Lay the plastic sheeting along the ground and up the wall/door you are protecting before placing the bags.
Place the sandbags on top of the plastic on the ground.
Lay the rest of the plastic over the outside (water side) of the sandbag wall.
Weigh down the top edge of the plastic with another row of sandbags.





Warning: Floodwater is often “blackwater,” meaning it is contaminated with chemicals or sewage.

Wear Gloves: Always wear heavy-duty gloves when removing bags that have been in contact with floodwater.
Do Not Reuse Sand: If the bags were touched by floodwater, the sand is likely contaminated. Do not use it for playgrounds or sandboxes.
Safe Disposal: Follow local municipal guidelines for disposing of “disaster debris”.

title: Power Outages
text:
Goal: To maintain essential life-safety systems, preserve food pantry refrigeration, and provide a reliable “charging station” and refuge for neighbors when the local grid fails.



Hardening your electrical system ensures that backup power can be used safely and efficiently.

Critical Circuit Identification: Audit your electrical panel. Use permanent labels to identify “Life Safety” and “Mission Critical” circuits.
Examples: Emergency lighting, sump pumps, food pantry refrigerators/freezers, and HVAC blowers.
Transfer Switch Installation: This is a safety requirement. Install a manual or automatic transfer switch to allow a generator to be safely connected to the building’s wiring without “backfeeding” into the utility grid, which protects line workers.
LED Retrofitting: Replace all high-draw incandescent or fluorescent lighting with LEDs. This significantly lowers the total electrical “load,” allowing a smaller, less expensive generator or battery to power more of the building.
Backup Power Options:

Generators: Choose between portable or permanent standby units.
Solar + Battery Storage: Evaluate a solar array paired with battery backup. This can provide indefinite power for small loads (office, cell-charging) during the day and can reduce monthly utility bills.






If you rely on a generator, your “run time” is only as good as your fuel supply.

Fuel Strategy: - Propane (LPG): If possible, prioritize propane. It does not degrade over time like gasoline and is safer to store long-term.

Gasoline/Diesel: If using liquid fuels, establish a strict rotation plan. Use high-quality fuel stabilizers to prevent engine clogs.

Passive Resilience: During a power outage, keep refrigerators and freezers closed. A full freezer can keep food safe for 48 hours if the door remains sealed.





A church’s greatest asset in a blackout is its ability to provide a sense of normalcy and safety to the neighborhood.

The “Charging Station” Protocol: Designate an area (such as the Fellowship Hall) with accessible power strips. Advertise this as a community resource for charging phones and medical devices.
Medically Vulnerable Outreach:

Identification: Develop a specific plan for congregants who are medically vulnerable (e.g., those using oxygen concentrators or refrigerated medication).
Refuge Status: Prepare to volunteer the church property as a formal refuge if the facility retains power (via generator or solar) while the surrounding community is in the dark.






Backup systems are prone to “shelf failure” if not tested regularly.



Medical Registry Update: Update the confidential list of parishioners who require electricity for medical reasons to ensure they are checked on first.
Load Test: Run the generator under a “full load” (powering the actual circuits it’s intended for) for at least 30 minutes.
Fuel Quality: Drain and replace any gasoline that is more than 6–12 months old, even if treated with stabilizer.

title: Hail Protection
text:
Goal: To protect the roof, expensive HVAC systems, and church vehicles from high-velocity impact damage.



For churches in North Texas, hail isn’t a “once-in-a-decade” event—it’s an annual certainty.

Frequency: Counties such as Dallas, Denton, Tarrant, and Collin experience an average of nine hailstorms per year.
Financial Impact: These storms result in annual losses exceeding $340 million, consistently ranking this region as the most affected in the nation.





The baseline for financial and physical stewardship.



Policy Audit: Make certain that the insurance policy explicitly covers hail damage. Verify the deductible specifically for wind/hail, as these are often higher than standard “all-peril” deductibles. Ensure “Replacement Cost Value” (RCV) is selected over “Actual Cash Value” (ACV) to avoid being underfunded during a total loss.






Physical upgrades to reduce the severity of impact damage.

HVAC Hail Guards: Install heavy-duty metal mesh “hail guards” over the delicate cooling fins of external air conditioning units. These allow airflow while preventing ice stones from flattening the fins and killing the unit’s efficiency.
Skylight Reinforcement: Replace standard plastic or thin-glass skylights with high-impact polycarbonate versions. Polycarbonate is virtually unbreakable under standard hail conditions.
Vehicle Shelter: If the church operates a ministry van or bus, designate a protected parking area. If a garage is unavailable, invest in a heavy-duty portable canopy or “hail blankets” for the fleet.





High-impact investments for when it is time to reroof or build new.



Class 4 Impact-Resistant Shingles: When reroofing, prioritize Class 4 rated shingles. These are tested to withstand a 2-inch steel ball dropped from 20 feet without cracking.
Metal Roofing: Consider transitioning to a metal roof. While the upfront cost is higher, the longevity and impact resistance are superior.
Note: Many insurance providers offer significant premium discounts (up to 20-30%) for installing Class 4 or metal roofing.




If the congregation is planning a new building or a major addition, integrate these “Built-In” defenses:

Steeper Roof Pitch: Steeper roofs allow hail to strike at an angle (glancing blow) rather than a direct 90-degree impact, significantly reducing the force.
Impact-Resistant Design: Specify impact-resistant skylights and reinforced siding (such as fiber cement) during the design phase.





Consult the Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety (IBHS) for a list of tested and approved impact-resistant roofing.

title: Wildfire and Smoke
text:
Goal: To prevent building ignition from wind-blown embers and maintain “Clean Air” zones to protect vulnerable residents from hazardous smoke.



These “once-and-done” structural improvements significantly increase the chance of a building surviving a wildfire without active intervention.

Fire Department Audit: Invite your local fire department to conduct a professional wildfire risk audit. They can provide specific countermeasures tailored to your local terrain.
Roofing & Eaves: Evaluate roofing material to ensure it is non-combustible (e.g., metal, tile, or Class A asphalt). Ensure all eaves are “boxed” or closed to prevent embers from being trapped.
Siding & Vents: Replace combustible siding with fire-resistant materials (fiber cement, brick, stone). Cover all external vents with fine wire mesh.
Propane Safety: Ensure any propane tanks are located at least 15 feet away from any building structure.
Evacuation Planning: Develop a formal evacuation plan that includes primary and secondary routes for the congregation and any weekday programs (like daycares).





Wildfire defense is about managing “fuel” in concentric circles around the church.



Remove Combustibles: Remove all mulch, woody shrubs, and debris within 5 feet of the church walls.
Hardscaping: Replace vegetation in this zone with gravel, stone, or concrete to prevent fire from touching the building.
Roof & Gutters: Ensure roof valleys and gutters are cleared of pine needles and leaves, which are the primary ignition points for embers.





Minimize Vegetation: Keep this area well-irrigated and free of dead wood.
Tree Grooming: Trim trees so that no branches are lower than 15 feet from the ground to prevent “ladder fires” from reaching the canopy.





Thinning: Remove the most flammable vegetation (such as junipers or dry brush) and create “breaks” between clusters of trees to slow a fire’s spread.






During regional fires, the church can serve as a “Clean Air Center” for those with respiratory issues or those without air conditioning.

HVAC Filtration: Upgrade your system to handle MERV 13 or HEPA filters. These are high-density filters capable of scrubbing fine smoke particles (PM2.5) from the air.
Portable Air Scrubbers: Purchase industrial-grade portable air purifiers. These can be deployed in the “Fellowship Hall” or Sanctuary to create a safe breathing environment even if the main HVAC is limited.
DIY Air Scrubbing: There are also plans for DIY air purifiers made from a box fan, MERV 13 filters, and duct tape that are surprisingly effective.
Monitoring: Keep a digital AQI (Air Quality Index) monitor on-site to verify that the indoor air remains safe when outdoor conditions are hazardous.





Consistency is key to fire safety. Add these tasks to your church’s seasonal calendar.

Vegetation Management: Trim back spring growth and clear brush near buildings every year before the dry season.
Gutter Maintenance: Clean gutters frequently or install fire-rated gutter covers to prevent debris accumulation.
Evacuation Drills: Conduct an annual drill to ensure staff and volunteers know how to clear the building quickly and safely.
Fire Department Audit: Invite your local fire department to conduct a professional wildfire risk audit.

title: Wind & Storm Safety
text:
Goal: Secure the building’s exterior “envelope” to prevent structural failure and minimize damage from wind-borne projectiles.



These items are the highest priority for life safety and protecting the structural integrity of the sanctuary.



Hurricane Straps: Install “hurricane straps” or clips to securely anchor the roof rafters to the wall plates. This ensures the roof stays attached during extreme uplift.
Signage: Install clear, permanent signage directing the congregation and visitors to designated Sheltered Areas (basements or interior rooms) for tornado safety.
Worst-Case Scenario Plan: Develop a formal protocol for a tornado approaching specifically during a Sunday service or high-occupancy event.

Who gives the order to move?
Which ushers guide which sections?
How do we assist those with limited mobility?






Tree Safety Audit: Have a professional check for dead limbs. Remove “hazard trees” that could fall on the building or power lines.
Roof Inspection: Check for damaged, loose, or missing shingles. Ensure shingles are rated for high-wind speeds (Class F or H).
Projectile Protocol: Review the plan for securing or moving exterior items (memorial benches, signage, trash cans, or playground equipment) when a high-wind warning is issued.






These measures provide a significant secondary layer of safety for the building’s occupants and internal assets.



Door Reinforcement: Install heavy-duty deadbolts. For double-entry doors, ensure “flush bolts” are installed at the top and bottom of the “inactive” leaf to prevent doors from blowing open.
Interior Stabilization: Secure tall furniture (such as heavy bookcases or storage cabinets) to the wall using earthquake-style straps.
Note: High winds can cause enough structural vibration to topple unanchored items, even if the building itself remains standing.






For congregations in high-risk zones seeking maximum protection.



Window Protection: Apply security window film (8–12 mil thickness) to high-risk glass areas. This keeps glass from shattering inward even if struck by debris.
Impact-Resistant Glass: Replace standard windows with impact-rated glass designed to withstand high-velocity debris strikes.
Storm Shutters: Install permanent storm shutters that can be deployed quickly before an event.
Plywood Stockpile: In hurricane-prone regions, pre-cut and store plywood sheets labeled for specific vulnerable windows.





Supply Audit: Annually verify that stockpiled supplies (plywood, fasteners, tools) are in good condition and haven’t succumbed to rot or rust.





Refer to FEMA Wind Resilience Guides for technical specifications on load paths and wind-resistant construction.

title: From Vision to Action
section: The Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task ForceThe Episcopal Diocese of Texas
text:
The Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task ForceThe Episcopal Diocese of Texas
Building on the foundation laid by the 2022–2024 Creation Care Task Force, a new team was commissioned in September 2025 to turn recommendations into reality. Our mission is to provide congregations with practical tools and theological grounding needed to lead in a changing world.
Preparation is about more than just technical steps. We invite you to explore these real-world stories from throughout our diocese, highlighting how others are putting resilience into practice, allowing us to better relate to our fellow travelers and their experiences. Additionally, if you have a story to share, please contact us. We want to help spread stories of both successes and failures to all our congregations.
We focus our efforts in three vital areas: Mitigation, Adaptation, and Formation.

Mitigation
Lowering our impact. Mitigation is about slowing the progress of climate change by shrinking our environmental footprint. This includes practical steps to lower energy consumption, conserve water, and move toward zero-waste communities.
    Goal: To be better stewards of the resources we use daily.


Adaptation
Building resilience. While we work to slow climate change, we must also prepare for its effects. Adaptation involves taking proactive measures to reduce our vulnerability to climate-related challenges and ensuring our congregations remain safe, prepared, and resilient.
    Goal: To protect our people and our properties in a shifting climate.


Formation
Re-imagining our relationship with Earth. Meaningful change involves emotion, thought, and action. We seek to engage all three as our community consciously considers our relationship with all of God’s Creation, moving away from a mindset of domination and toward acts of loving and responsible stewardship.
    Goal: To honor the Creator by loving and protecting every aspect of God’s creation.

Resource Links

Zero Waste Church: A North Carolina Episcopal Church with a website filled with potential projects for reducing waste.
Keep Texas Beautiful: An organization dedicated to litter cleanup, with guides for group activities and other resources.

For more information, contact
Alan Jackson and Robin Bullington .

title: Extreme Heat Resilience
text:
Goal: To protect parishioners from heat-related illness, maintain facility efficiency, and provide a refuge for the vulnerable during prolonged high-heat events.



Heat waves cause more fatalities each year than any other weather-related event.

Texas vs. Europe: In 2022, the European heat wave resulted in approximately 62,000 deaths, while Texas saw at least 306. The difference is largely attributed to the near-universality of air conditioning in Texas—making the reliability of our A/C systems a matter of life and death.





Essential steps to ensure the facility remains functional and safe.



Intelligent Thermostats: Install smart or programmable thermostats to manage cooling cycles efficiently and reduce strain on the grid.
Efficiency Note: Energy used for cooling increases as the square of the temperature difference. To cool a facility down to 80℉ when the outside temperature is 100℉ costs four times as much as when the outside temperature is 90℉.





A/C System Audit: Perform professional annual maintenance on all air conditioning units before the summer peak to ensure they can handle sustained high-load operation.






Measures to improve energy efficiency and protect the most vulnerable.



Window Management: Cover windows with drapes, shades, or specific heat-reflecting window films to block solar gain.
Building Envelope: Weather-strip all doors and windows and add insulation to keep heat out and cool air in.
Ventilation: Use powered attic ventilators or fans to clear hot air from the building’s attic.
Audit: Have a professional energy audit performed to identify “leaks” in the building’s efficiency.





Vulnerable Registry: Update the list of parishioners who live without A/C, focusing specifically on the elderly or infirm.
Activity Adjustment: Establish a policy to curtail all outside church activity, including landscape maintenance, during heat alerts.






Advanced strategies for long-term resilience and neighborhood support.



Reflective Surfaces: Use heat-reflective shingles when replacing the roof or explore “cool pavement” options for the parking lot to reduce the heat-island effect.
The Cooling Center Model: Formally prepare the church to serve as a community cooling center, potentially including battery backup to keep systems running if the grid fails.
Tree Canopy: Partner with local groups to plant trees in the neighborhood to provide natural shade and cooling.





Financial Assistance: Establish a fund to help parishioners with high power bills during extreme heat events.
Housing Coordination: Help vulnerable people find temporary air-conditioned housing or “buddy” them with parishioners who have reliable cooling.

Activity Adjustment: Establish a policy to curtail all outside church activity, including landscape maintenance, during heat alerts.

title: Extreme Heat Resilience
text:
Advanced strategies for long-term resilience and neighborhood support.



Reflective Surfaces: Use heat-reflective shingles when replacing the roof or explore “cool pavement” options for the parking lot to reduce the heat-island effect.
The Cooling Center Model: Formally prepare the church to serve as a community cooling center, potentially including battery backup to keep systems running if the grid fails.
Tree Canopy: Partner with local groups to plant trees in the neighborhood to provide natural shade and cooling.





Financial Assistance: Establish a fund to help parishioners with high power bills during extreme heat events.
Housing Coordination: Help vulnerable people find temporary air-conditioned housing or “buddy” them with parishioners who have reliable cooling.

title: Liturgy for Planting
text:
A Celebration of Creation Resilience


The community gathers around the planting site. The officiant begins:
Officiant: The earth is the Lord’s and all that is in it.
People: The world and all who dwell therein.
Officiant: For God has founded it upon the seas and established it upon the rivers.
People: We gather today to do our part in caring for the earth that God has entrusted to us.
Officiant: Almighty God, in giving us responsibility for things on earth, you made us fellow workers in your creation: Give us wisdom and reverence so to use the resources of nature, that no one may suffer from our abuse of them, and that generations yet to come may continue to praise you for your bounty; through Jesus Christ our Lord. Amen. (BCP Prayers and Thanksgivings 41. For the Conservation of Natural Resources, edited) [cite: 1]



A member of the congregation reads from the book of Genesis (1:27-30, the Message).
God spoke: “Let us make human beings in our image, make them reflect our nature so they can be responsible for the fish in the sea, the birds in the air, the cattle, And, yes, Earth itself, and every animal that moves on the face of Earth.”
God created human beings; he created them godlike, reflecting God’s nature. He created them male and female. God blessed them: “Prosper! Reproduce! Fill Earth! Take charge! Be responsible for fish in the sea and birds in the air, for every living thing that moves on the face of Earth.”



Officiant: Gracious Lord, you created the earth and filled it with all manner of living organisms. They form ecosystems according to your design, with each member striving to achieve your goal. You created mankind as stewards to tend your magnificent gardens for the benefit of all beings, great and small. You appointed each of us to do our part in maintaining the balance of nature so that we may live in peace and harmony with all our brothers and sisters, sharing in the bounty of Your creation.
Bless our hands as we gently tend Your garden.
People: May our humble efforts serve to glorify Your creation.
Officiant: Soil is much more than an aggregate of particles of sand, small stones, clay, and organic matter. Proper garden soil is alive with various organisms of different sizes and functions, along with a balance of beneficial nutrients.
Guide our efforts as we prepare this soil to provide nourishment and support for these plants to thrive.
People: May our labor strengthen this garden in its ability to nourish these plants.
Officiant: Heavenly Father, your precious gift of water sustains all life. Since the beginning of creation, when the Holy Spirit moved over it, all living things have been forever linked through its wondrous properties.
May our knowledge and skills ensure that this garden is properly prepared to utilize the water provided by nature and require minimal intervention from its stewards.
People: May our humble efforts serve to maintain balance in nature.
Officiant: Gracious Heavenly Father, by Your Word, every plant was created to be an integral part of Your garden. We all suffer when the balance and harmony in our environment are disrupted.
We thank you for these native plants/trees, designed by your hand to thrive in this portion of your creation for which we are the chosen stewards.
People: May they thrive and strengthen the bonds between us and your creation.
Officiant: We bless this soil, that it may be a womb for growth. We bless this water, that it may be a source of strength. We bless these plants, that they may provide shade for the weary, oxygen for the living, and a home for the small creatures of the air.
People: Lord, make us faithful stewards of this place and gardeners of your grace.



As the tree is placed in the ground or the seeds are sown, the people may say together:
As we tend this garden of your creation, we commit ourselves to:

Shelter those who suffer from the heat of the world.
Conserve the precious gift of water.
Protect the diversity of the life you have made.
Hope for a future where all creation flourishes together.




Officiant: Go forth into the world as stewards of the Earth. Be rooted in love, growing in hope, and bearing the fruits of the Spirit.
People: Thanks be to God. Amen.






Note

title: Mitigation
text:
Goal: To achieve carbon neutrality in all facilities and operations by 2035, serving as a faithful example of environmental stewardship for our communities.



In July 2022, The Episcopal Church’s General Convention committed the entire church to reaching carbon neutrality by 2030. While the direct impact of a single congregation may seem small, our actions stand as vital examples that can inspire others to join in the work of healing God’s creation.




Before jumping into major projects like solar panels, it is vital to first understand your building’s current performance. Simple, low-cost solutions identified through audits often yield the highest immediate impact.



Energy Audit: Perform a professional energy audit to identify where heat is escaping and where electrical loads can be reduced.
Water Audit: Conduct a full water audit to find hidden leaks and inefficient fixtures.
Waste Audit: Identify opportunities to reduce landfill waste through composting, recycling, and eliminating single-use plastics.






These “easy wins” can be implemented quickly and often pay for themselves within months.

Lighting Retrofits: Replace all CFL or incandescent bulbs with LEDs. This can lower lighting electricity usage by 40%, and the bulbs last twice as long. (Be sure to use Energy Star certified bulbs)
Landscape Maintenance: Replace two-stroke, gasoline, and diesel landscaping maintenance equipment with electric equipment, or engage contractors using electric equipment.
Leak Detection: Test all toilets for leaks every 6 months. A single leaking toilet can cost up to $50 per month in wasted water.
Watering Systems: Adjust irrigation to only spray where needed and plant native trees to reduce the long-term need for watering.
Sustainable Hospitality: Encourage parishioners to contribute ceramic coffee mugs to replace single-use plastic cups.
Efficiency Upgrades:

Replace failed dishwashers with Energy Star models.
Install high-efficiency lavatory faucet aerators (.5 to 1.0 GPM).
Insulate all hot water lines with R-3 or higher insulation.






These projects require more planning but offer significant long-term savings.

Smart Climate Control: Install smart or programmable thermostats to optimize heating and cooling.
Building Envelope: Seal or replace leaky ductwork and add ceiling insulation where needed.
Mechanical Upgrades: Replace aging water heaters with high-efficiency heat pump models.
Fixture Upgrades: Replace standard toilets with low-flow models, upgrade faucets.
Resilient Landscaping: Transition to xeriscaping with native plants that can survive drought without supplemental watering.





These “hard” projects represent deep shifts in how your facility interacts with the environment.

HVAC Modernization: Replace end-of-life heating and cooling systems with the highest-efficiency equipment available.
Renewable Energy: Install a solar array to generate clean power on-site.
Water Stewardship: Install rainwater cisterns or graywater systems for landscape needs.
Clean Transportation: Install EV charging stations to support the transition to electric vehicles for staff and parishioners.

title: Adaptation
text:
Resilience & Adaptation: Safeguarding Our Mission
Natural disasters can disrupt our ministry, but proactive hardening ensures our doors stay open when the community needs us most. Here is our step-by-step approach to minimizing damage and maximizing safety.
The source and reference for most of this material may be found in the Disaster Mitigation document from the Task Force.

1. Identify Your Primary Risks
The first step in stewardship is understanding where we are most vulnerable. Risk is calculated by looking at the likelihood of an event combined with its potential severity.

Action: Review our Risk Matrices Document to see the initial estimates calculated by the Task Force. These prioritize which threats require our immediate attention.



2. Evaluate Potential Adaptations
Once we know the risks, we perform a cost/benefit analysis. We look for “high-impact, low-cost” wins first, followed by long-term capital investments. A good general source for useful information is the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety.
Select a category below to view specific hardening recommendations:



Disaster Basics
Heat Resilience
Wildfire Mitigation


Cold & Freezing
Flooding & Drainage
Wind & Storm Safety


Hail Protection
Lightning Protection
Power Outage Backup





3. Budget & Resource Allocation
We prioritize adaptations that offer the greatest protection for the most people. This isn’t just an expense; it is an investment in our future.

Funding the Work: Check our Potential Funding Sources page for information on grants, denominational aid, and community partnerships.



4. Implementation & Maintenance
A plan only works if it is put into motion. Our final phase involves:

Executing physical upgrades (e.g., roof reinforcement, sump pump installation).
Updating emergency response protocols.
Annual Review: Disasters change, and so should our plan.

title: Energy Audits
text:
Goal: To identify where our buildings are “leaking” energy and money, allowing us to redirect those resources toward mission and ministry.



The “greenest” kilowatt-hour is the one you never use. Most older church facilities can reduce their energy bills by 20–30% simply by identifying air leaks and optimizing aging HVAC systems.




Before spending money on a professional, your “Green Team” can identify the “low-hanging fruit” in a single afternoon.

The Draft Test: On a windy day, use a lit incense stick or a thin tissue near windows, doors, and electrical outlets. If the smoke or tissue flutters, you have an air leak that needs weatherstripping or caulk.
Lighting Inventory: Count every bulb in the building. If it isn’t an LED, mark it for replacement. Switching to LEDs is the single fastest ROI in energy stewardship.
The “Phantom Load” Search: Identify electronics that stay plugged in 24/7 (coffee makers, old CRT televisions, printers). Use power strips to truly turn them off when not in use.
Thermostat Audit: Ensure thermostats are not “fighting” each other and are programmed to drift (higher in summer, lower in winter) during hours the building is unoccupied.





Many local power providers offer free or low-cost basic assessments for commercial and non-profit customers.

CenterPoint Energy (Houston): Through their SCORE Program (Schools and Conserving Resources), CenterPoint provides energy performance benchmarking and technical assistance to help non-profits identify efficiency projects.
Austin Energy / CPS Energy: These utilities often provide free on-site energy walk-throughs and significant rebates for attic insulation and HVAC upgrades.
Why do this? Utility audits are excellent for securing rebates. They often pay for a portion of the hardware if you follow their specific efficiency recommendations.





The taskforce is currently running a few pilot audits with a well-regarded professional company that specializes in church audits. It is hoped that we can work out a deal for the diocese, which may include financing for congregations that are under-resourced. More to come in this space.
A comprehensive audit generally runs about $5,000.










Thermal Imaging: Using infrared cameras to see exactly where heat is entering or escaping through walls and ceilings.
HVAC Life-Cycle Analysis: A detailed report on when your units will likely fail and what high-efficiency models should replace them.
Project Prioritization: A data-backed list of projects ranked by their “Payback Period” (how long it takes for energy savings to pay for the upgrade).






CenterPoint Energy SCORE Program
Austin Energy Energy Conservation Audit
Energy Star: Action Workbook for Congregations

title: Agent Profile: Creation Care Technical Consultant
text:
You are the Creation Care Technical Consultant for the Episcopal Diocese of Texas (EDOT). Your goal is to support the “Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task Force” in moving from vision to action.



Theological Foundation: Grounded in “Sacramental Stewardship.” Use the language of “visible signs of inward grace” when discussing physical campus upgrades.
Tone: Professional, supportive, and adaptive. Use a touch of wit, but remain focused on the urgency of the climate mission.
Context: Be mindful that we are in “oil country.” Our mission is not antagonistic but transformative—modeling how a diocese linked to the energy industry can lead the way in Net Zero transitions.





The Diocese is working toward a Net Zero goal of 2035–2040. All advice should align with this timeline and the following three pillars: 1. Adaptation: Hardening properties against heat, wind, and flood. 2. Mitigation: Reducing footprints (LEDs, HVAC, Solar). 3. Formation: Breaking “climate silence” through liturgies and native landscaping.



When generating content or code, adhere to these rules: - Format: Default to Quarto Markdown (.qmd) using Bootstrap 5 styling. - Math/Science: Use LaTeX only for formal formulas (e.g., \(Carbon Footprint = \sum (Energy \times Emission Factor)\)). Use standard text for units like 180°C or 10%. - Linking: Assume all files reside in the same root directory. Use relative links: [Mitigation](mitigation.qmd). - Navigation: Every page must include a standard navigation bar linking back to index.qmd, mitigation.qmd, formation.qmd, and adaptation.qmd. - Formatting: When reformatting pages into markdown, do not change the content, but use what is provided verbatim.




Verification: Always prioritize data found in the “Website” Google Drive folder (ID: 14iYUr7i_xa_YBPlSIv9jt-xK0D43yVij).
Conciseness: Provide clear, scannable responses. Avoid “walls of text.”
Next Steps: Always end a response with a high-value suggestion for the next task (e.g., “Would you like me to draft the Water Audit page next?”).

title: Sustainable Hospitality
text:
Goal: To foster a culture of care for creation by reducing waste in our fellowship and community gatherings, moving toward a zero-waste parish life.



Every cup of coffee shared and every meal hosted is an opportunity to practice stewardship. By shifting away from single-use items, we reduce the amount of waste sent to landfills and demonstrate a commitment to a simpler, more intentional way of life.




Essential, low-cost steps to immediately reduce the environmental impact of church gatherings.



The Mug Ministry: Encourage parishioners to donate ceramic coffee mugs to the church. This eliminates the need for single-use plastic or Styrofoam cups during coffee hour.
Waste Education: Place clear signage near waste bins to help parishioners distinguish between recyclables, compost, and landfill items.





Eliminate Styrofoam: Formally ban the purchase and use of expanded polystyrene (Styrofoam) for all parish events.






Measures that improve the sustainability of the church’s hospitality ministry.



Bulk Serving: Transition from individual condiment packets (sugar, cream, salt) to bulk dispensers or bowls. This significantly reduces small-scale plastic waste.
Reusable Linens: Invest in cloth tablecloths and napkins that can be laundered rather than discarded.





“Bring Your Own” Encouragement: Regularly remind the congregation via the bulletin to bring their own reusable water bottles or coffee travel mugs to church events.






Long-term investments to fully integrate sustainability into the church’s hospitality infrastructure.



High-Efficiency Dishwashing: Install an Energy Star-rated commercial dishwasher to make using reusable plates and silverware more efficient for large groups.
Composting System: Establish an on-site composting system for food scraps from the parish kitchen, which can then be used in the church’s resilient landscaping or community garden.






Zero Waste Church Guide

title: Resilient Landscapes
text:
Goal: To transform church grounds into resilient ecosystems that conserve water, support local wildlife, and provide a cooling refuge for the neighborhood.
A Liturgy for the Planting of Trees and Gardens



As temperatures rise, our parking lots and rooftops often become “heat islands,” trapping warmth and driving up cooling costs. By rethinking our landscaping, we can lower local temperatures and reduce the strain on our building’s HVAC systems.


Planting native canopy trees is one of the most effective long-term investments a congregation can make.

The Heat Island Effect: Mature trees can reduce peak summer temperatures on asphalt and dark roofs by up to 20°F–45°F.
Energy Savings: Strategically planting deciduous trees on the south and west sides of the sanctuary provides shade in the summer while allowing sunlight to warm the building in the winter.
Diocesan Favorites: Consider native giants like the Live Oak, Cedar Elm, or Pecan for long-lasting shade and resilience.






Standard turf grass is often the most water-intensive and least resilient part of a church property. We advocate for a transition toward more sustainable models:


Xeriscaping is not “zero-scaping” (rocks and sand); it is a method of landscaping that reduces or eliminates the need for supplemental water.

Soil Preparation: Improving soil with organic compost helps it retain moisture longer.
Zoning: Grouping plants with similar water needs together to prevent over-watering.
Mulching: Using thick layers of wood chips or pine bark to cool the soil and prevent evaporation.




Plants that are indigenous to our part of Texas have spent thousands of years adapting to our specific cycle of floods and droughts.

Low Maintenance: Once established, native plants require little to no fertilizer or pesticides.
Biodiversity: They provide essential food and habitat for local pollinators, birds, and butterflies.
Resilience: Unlike exotic ornamentals, native species are more likely to survive extreme “Blue Norther” freezes and summer heat waves.









Audit Your Irrigation: Check for broken sprinkler heads and ensure timers are set to water in the early morning to minimize evaporation.
Identify Hazard Trees: Ensure current trees are healthy and not at risk of falling on power lines or buildings during storms.





Mulch New Growth: Apply 3 inches of mulch to all flower beds and around the base of trees to protect root systems from the sun.
Native Conversion: Identify one small area of “unused” turf grass to convert into a native wildflower or pollinator garden.





Permeable Paving: If resurfacing the parking lot, consider permeable pavers that allow rainwater to soak into the ground rather than running off into the storm drains.
Community Garden: Use resilient landscaping techniques to grow food for local pantries, demonstrating sustainable agricultural practices.






Native Plant Society of Texas: Guides on which species thrive in your specific county.
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center: An extensive database of Texas-native plants and trees.
Texas A & M - Texas Tree Planting Guide: The Texas Tree Planting Guide helps individuals select the best trees for the needs as well as provides helpful information for planting and care.
Certified Wildlife Habitat: Learn about creating a wildlife-friendly space.

title: Resilient Landscapes
text:
As temperatures rise, our parking lots and rooftops often become “heat islands,” trapping warmth and driving up cooling costs. By rethinking our landscaping, we can lower local temperatures and reduce the strain on our building’s HVAC systems.


Planting native canopy trees is one of the most effective long-term investments a congregation can make.

The Heat Island Effect: Mature trees can reduce peak summer temperatures on asphalt and dark roofs by up to 20°F–45°F.
Energy Savings: Strategically planting deciduous trees on the south and west sides of the sanctuary provides shade in the summer while allowing sunlight to warm the building in the winter.
Diocesan Favorites: Consider native giants like the Live Oak, Cedar Elm, or Pecan for long-lasting shade and resilience.

title: Resilient Landscapes
text:
Standard turf grass is often the most water-intensive and least resilient part of a church property. We advocate for a transition toward more sustainable models:


Xeriscaping is not “zero-scaping” (rocks and sand); it is a method of landscaping that reduces or eliminates the need for supplemental water.

Soil Preparation: Improving soil with organic compost helps it retain moisture longer.
Zoning: Grouping plants with similar water needs together to prevent over-watering.
Mulching: Using thick layers of wood chips or pine bark to cool the soil and prevent evaporation.




Plants that are indigenous to our part of Texas have spent thousands of years adapting to our specific cycle of floods and droughts.

Low Maintenance: Once established, native plants require little to no fertilizer or pesticides.
Biodiversity: They provide essential food and habitat for local pollinators, birds, and butterflies.
Resilience: Unlike exotic ornamentals, native species are more likely to survive extreme “Blue Norther” freezes and summer heat waves.

title: Resilient Landscapes
text:
Audit Your Irrigation: Check for broken sprinkler heads and ensure timers are set to water in the early morning to minimize evaporation.
Identify Hazard Trees: Ensure current trees are healthy and not at risk of falling on power lines or buildings during storms.





Mulch New Growth: Apply 3 inches of mulch to all flower beds and around the base of trees to protect root systems from the sun.
Native Conversion: Identify one small area of “unused” turf grass to convert into a native wildflower or pollinator garden.





Permeable Paving: If resurfacing the parking lot, consider permeable pavers that allow rainwater to soak into the ground rather than running off into the storm drains.
Community Garden: Use resilient landscaping techniques to grow food for local pantries, demonstrating sustainable agricultural practices.






Native Plant Society of Texas: Guides on which species thrive in your specific county.
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center: An extensive database of Texas-native plants and trees.
Texas A & M - Texas Tree Planting Guide: The Texas Tree Planting Guide helps individuals select the best trees for the needs as well as provides helpful information for planting and care.
Certified Wildlife Habitat: Learn about creating a wildlife-friendly space.

title: Formation
text:
Goal: To move beyond the “climate silence” in our pews and reclaim our role as stewards of God’s handiwork.



There is a persistent myth that people in our pews aren’t concerned about our changing climate. However, data suggests we are suffering from a “perception gap” rather than a lack of concern.

The Reality: 72% of Americans are concerned about climate change.
The Perception: Most people believe only 52% of their neighbors care.
The Result: To avoid perceived conflict, we stop talking about it. This silence inadvertently feeds the myth that the majority doesn’t care, when the opposite is true. The reality is that “climate dismissives” are a small, albeit vocal, minority.

We strive to encourage loving and practical conversation about the challenges of climate change and environmental degradation, informed by accurate information and abundant civility.




Our clergy are often the most prepared to lead this conversation, yet the most hesitant to start it.

Broad Consensus: Nearly 90% of clergy acknowledge that climate change is real.
Human Agency: 60% believe humans play a significant role, while another 30% believe humans play a lesser but still present role.
The Discussion Gap: Despite these beliefs, only half of clergy have discussed the topic with their congregations, and only 25% have addressed it more than once or twice.

We strive to encourage our clergy to actively help create a space where faith and science meet in service to God’s Creation.




We have curated a library of tools to help your congregation engage with Creation Theology at every level.



Preaching Creation: Homiletic resources for integrating stewardship into the liturgical year.
Visionary Leadership: Templates for leading vestries and boards through “green” transitions.





God’s Gardeners: Sunday School curricula focused on the wonder of nature.
Eco-Justice for Teens: Discussion guides for connecting faith with environmental advocacy.





Book Club Recommendations: View our curated list of books on theology, ecology, and hope—complete with reviews and discussion prompts.
Adult Formation Series: Multi-week curricula exploring the biblical foundations of “Creation Care.”






Not every conversation needs to start in a classroom. Sometimes, the best theology is practiced with our hands in the dirt and our feet on the earth.

Stewardship of the Land: Resources for water-wise gardening, xeriscaping, and planting native species on church grounds and at home.
Plalking (Pick-up + Walking): A spiritual practice of “walking and picking up”—caring for the neighborhood while caring for the soul.
Sacred Excursions: Birding and nature walks designed as “contemplative excursions” to reconnect with the local environment.

title: Flooding & Drainage
text:
Goal: To prevent water from entering church structures, maintain the integrity of our foundations, and ensure our facility remains a dry, safe haven for the community during and after a flood event.



Before implementing physical changes, we must understand the “lay of the land”.

Floodplain Mapping: Identify where our buildings sit relative to the 100-year (1% annual chance) and 500-year (0.2% annual chance) floodplains.
Historical Data: Consult local archives to find the “high-water mark” of the last major local flood.
Entry Point Inspection: Walk the perimeter to identify every possible water entry point, including basement windows, utility conduits, and low-threshold doors.





These are physical modifications to the building and grounds to mitigate damage.



Dry Floodproofing: For essential occupied spaces, we aim to make the building watertight. This involves applying waterproof membranes to exterior walls and installing “Flood Shields” (removable barriers) for doors.
⚠️ Critical Maintenance Note: The Weep-Hole Caveat While sealing the building envelope, extreme caution must be exercised regarding weep-holes in masonry or brick veneer. These small gaps are vital for allowing moisture to escape from the wall cavity to prevent rot and mold.
During a Flood: Weep-holes must be temporarily sealed (using specialized flood-rated plugs or shields) to prevent floodwater from entering the wall assembly. Sheets of plastic held by sandbags, or peelable caulk.
Post-Flood: These plugs must be removed immediately once the water recedes. Permanently plugging weep-holes in a misguided attempt at waterproofing will trap condensation inside your walls, leading to structural decay and severe indoor air quality issues.
Wet Floodproofing: For crawlspaces or unfinished basements, we may choose to allow water in to equalize pressure and prevent foundation collapse. This requires installing Hydrostatic Flood Vents and using flood-resistant materials (concrete, tile).
Backflow Prevention: Install check valves on all sewer and floor drain lines to prevent “blackwater” (sewage) from backing up into the church during heavy rains.





Strategic Elevation: Elevate all A/C compressors, furnaces, and electrical panels. While the goal is the Base Flood Elevation (BFE) + 1 foot, at minimum, systems should be raised above the 10–15 year flood level.
Sump Pump Redundancy: Every basement must have a primary sump pump and a battery-powered backup. In high-risk areas, a third “water-powered” backup pump is recommended.






We must manage how water moves across our property to protect both our buildings and our neighbors downstream.

Precision Grading: The ground must slope away from foundations at a minimum of 1 inch per foot for the first 6–10 feet.
Diversionary Infrastructure: - Channels & Swales: Construct parabolic or trapezoidal channels to redirect water around buildings rather than toward them.

Levees: For river-adjacent properties, consider a low-profile earthen levee, designed with a 3:1 slope for stability and maintenance.

High-Capacity Drainage: Upgrade to 6-inch gutters and ensure downspouts discharge at least 10 feet away from the foundation into stabilized areas.
Permeable Parking: When repaving, use permeable pavers or porous asphalt to allow the ground to absorb runoff, reducing the burden on municipal storm drains.





Hardening is only half the battle; we must be ready to act when a “Watch” or “Warning” is issued.



Sandbagging Strategy: If sandbags are part of our plan, follow a “Staggered Brick” pattern with a plastic liner underneath to create a watertight seal.
Asset Protection: Move high-value items (organs, historical records, sound equipment) to the second floor or the highest point in the sanctuary.
Relocation Evaluation: In cases of extreme and repetitive flooding, the leadership must evaluate the long-term viability of the site and consider the “Managed Retreat” to higher ground.





Debris Clearance: Inspect and clear all storm drains, ditches, and diversionary channels.
Sandbag Audit: Check stored sandbags for rot or UV damage.
Evacuation Drills: Train volunteers on how to assist at-risk congregants or neighbors with limited mobility during a flood evacuation.







FEMA P-348: Protecting Building Utility Systems – The definitive guide for utility hardening.
The Silver Jackets – State-led interagency teams that can provide local flood risk data.
How to Use Sandbags - brief guide on sand-bagging tech.

title: Stories
text:
Goal: To share real-world examples of how congregations are putting resilience and creation care into practice, allowing us to learn from each other’s successes and challenges.



Preparation is about more than technical steps; it’s about the people and communities leading the way. These stories highlight the practical application of our shared mission to protect God’s creation.




Stories of congregations reducing their environmental impact and operational costs.


After installing solar panels in July 2024, Christ Episcopal has seen a dramatic shift in its stewardship of resources.

The Impact: Nearly two years later, church leaders report that the system is making a major impact on both their carbon footprint and their bottom line.
Financial Stewardship: The transition has allowed the parish to save hundreds of dollars on monthly utility costs, redirecting those funds toward their core ministries.
Read the full story here.




In late 2022, St. Stephen’s Episcopal School in Austin, Texas, completed its “Let the Sunshine In” initiative, installing 1,064 solar panels across its 370-acre campus to generate approximately 787,594 kWh of clean energy annually. Supported by lead donors and Austin Energy rebates, the project is projected to save the school $65,000 in yearly utility costs while doubling as a “living laboratory” where students can study the science, ethics, and economics of renewable energy. By integrating this infrastructure into both its operations and its curriculum, St. Stephen’s has created a visible, long-term witness to the Episcopal Church’s commitment to the Care of Creation, modeling how institutional stewardship can foster both financial resilience and environmental responsibility. Read the full story here.



In an effort to align its historic urban presence with modern environmental stewardship, St. David’s Episcopal Church in Austin, Texas, integrated a significant solar array as part of its major four-story facility expansion. By installing photovoltaic panels atop its downtown campus, the parish has successfully harnessed renewable energy to offset the high electrical demands of its large-scale hospitality spaces and commercial-grade kitchen. This commitment to solar power serves as a visible witness to creation care, demonstrating how historic congregations can utilize rooftops for climate mitigation while strengthening their long-term financial resilience through reduced utility expenditures. Read the full story here.



In a significant move toward institutional resilience, St. Thomas the Apostle Episcopal Church in Houston, Texas, has committed to installing a 52 kW solar array through a partnership with RE-volv, marking the 20th state reached by the nonprofit’s clean energy mission. Rev. Mike Stone describes the investment as a fundamental act of environmental stewardship that creates an “economy of scale” for the benefit of the local neighborhood. The system is projected to save the congregation over $120,753 in energy costs, funds that will be directly reinvested into community outreach—specifically supporting the church’s food security initiatives that provide fresh food to more than 500 people each month. By leveraging solar power to reduce operational overhead, St. Thomas is effectively transforming renewable energy into tangible local impact, demonstrating how climate mitigation and social mission can work hand-in-hand. Read the full story here





Stories of hardening our facilities and caring for our neighbors during extreme events.



The Challenge: Has your parish faced a flood, freeze, or heatwave?
The Action: What steps did you take to protect your building or serve as a community refuge?
The Lesson: What is one thing you wish you had known before the event?






Stories of spiritual growth and community engagement with the natural world.



The Practice: Does your congregation host a “Book Club,” a “Liturgy in the Garden,” or a “Creation Walk”?
The Transformation: How has focusing on stewardship changed the way your members view their faith and their local ecosystem?






We want to spread stories of both successes and failures to all our congregations. Sharing your experience helps your “fellow travelers” better relate to the work of resilience.
If you have a story, a photo, or a lesson learned to share, please contact Alan Jackson and Robin Bullington

title: Disaster Basics
text:
Goal: To establish the core frameworks of communication, authority, and documentation required to manage any crisis effectively.



These foundational items represent the core framework without which other disaster responses will likely fail.



The Preparedness Workbook: Work through the formal Congregation Emergency Preparedness Workbook to establish your baseline.
Written Plans: Create a specific written response plan for your congregation’s most likely disasters (e.g., hurricanes, heat, wildfire, tornado, or flooding).
Communication Strategy: Establish a robust parish communication strategy, such as an automated texting platform, to reach parishioners before and after an event.
Utility Access: Identify exactly where and how to turn off water, gas, and electricity. Ensure any specialized tools (like a meter key) are labeled and on-site.
Emergency Signage: Audit all “Exit” signs and directional signage to ensure they clearly guide people to safety during a crisis.
Digital Readiness: Ensure all staff download the FEMA emergency app and enable emergency weather alerts on their mobile devices.





Disaster Coordinator: Appoint a designated disaster coordinator annually (preferably a lay leader rather than the rector) to oversee the plan.
Insurance Audit: Verify that all insurance policies (including flood, fire, and wind) are current and provide sufficient coverage.
Data Protection: Conduct a backup test to ensure that critical parish records are not only backed up to the cloud but are actually accessible and recoverable.
Communication Drill: Test your communication plan annually by updating the parish contact list and sending a test broadcast.
Detection Systems: Test all smoke and carbon monoxide detectors throughout the facility.






These measures provide an additional layer of preparedness for the staff and the wider congregation.



Parishioner Training: Host annual education sessions to help individual parishioners and families develop their own personal emergency preparedness plans.
Work-from-Home Drill: Conduct a “work-from-home” day for staff to test the congregation’s ability to maintain operations and secure buildings remotely.






Advanced strategies for extending the church’s mission into the community and building a culture of mutual aid.
The Resilience Truth: Every disaster teaches that survival correlates less with isolation than with social cohesion. In a crisis, it is not the loner who fares best, but the block, town, or network that has learned to work together.



Social Cohesion Mapping: Proactively “know your neighbors.” Identify the strengths and needs of the blocks immediately surrounding the church property before a crisis occurs.
Evacuation Networks: Coordinate with neighboring parishes and civic groups to develop evacuation plans specifically for those lacking personal transportation or secure shelter.
Local Partnerships: Establish formal relationships with local emergency management and first responders. Offer the church as a designated “Refugia”—a reliable heating/cooling center or a central supply distribution point.
Vulnerability Planning: Develop targeted outreach plans to assist the elderly, the infirm, and those with limited mobility in your neighborhood. Ensure they have a clear path to safety during evacuations or extreme weather events.





Mutual Aid Training: Host annual neighborhood workshops on basic disaster prep to build trust and familiarity between the congregation and the surrounding community.
Network Testing: Review your partnership agreements with local NGOs and neighboring parishes to ensure contact information and resource lists are current.






The Congregation Emergency Preparedness Workbook
FEMA Mobile App
Ready.gov: Disaster Preparedness Tips

title: Waste Audit & Reduction
text:
Goal: To minimize the church’s environmental footprint by systematically identifying, reducing, and diverting waste from landfills, honoring God’s creation through mindful consumption.



Waste is often a sign of “system leakage”—resources bought and paid for that end up as environmental liabilities.

The Methane Problem: Organic waste in landfills produces methane, a greenhouse gas significantly more potent than CO2.
The Cost of Convenience: Single-use plastics and high-volume trash hauling are recurring expenses that can be redirected toward mission and ministry.






Foundational steps to understand your current waste stream.



The 24-Hour Sort: Collect 24 hours’ worth of typical parish waste. With gloves and a tarp, sort the bags into three categories: True Trash, Recyclables, and Organics/Compostables.
Visual Baseline: Note the “contamination rate”—how much recyclable material is currently being thrown in the trash, and how much trash is in the recycling bins.
Contract Review: Examine your current waste hauling contract. Are you paying for “pulls” (pickups) of half-empty dumpsters? Adjust the frequency to match your actual needs.





Single-Use Audit: Identify the top 3 single-use plastic items used by the parish (e.g., water bottles, plastic forks, Styrofoam cups) and set a date to transition to reusable or compostable alternatives.






Measures to improve efficiency and cultural habits.



The “Twin Bin” System: Ensure that every trash can on campus is paired with a clearly labeled recycling bin. Bins should be color-coded and feature icons (images of bottles/paper) rather than just text.
Bulk Purchasing: Transition from individual-sized items (milk carafes, sugar packets, bottled water) to bulk dispensers in the kitchen and parish hall.





Waste Education: Host a “Trash Talk” during coffee hour to show parishioners exactly what can and cannot be recycled in your specific municipality.
Signage Audit: Replace faded or confusing signs. Use photos of actual items used in the parish to eliminate guesswork.






Advanced strategies for total waste diversion and neighborhood impact.



On-Site Composting: Establish a “Bokashi” or traditional compost bin for kitchen scraps. The resulting “black gold” can be used to fertilize the church’s Resilient Landscaping.
TerraCycle Hub: Become a community drop-off point for “hard-to-recycle” items not accepted by city bins (e.g., snack wrappers, alkaline batteries, electronics, or light bulbs).





Zero-Waste Events: Commit to making the Parish Picnic or Annual Meeting “Zero-Waste” by using only reusables or certified compostables and providing a “sorting station” staffed by youth volunteers.
Circular Economy Partnership: Partner with local schools or community gardens to provide compost or to source “ugly” produce that would otherwise be wasted.






EPA: How to Start a Waste Reduction Program
TerraCycle: Recycling the “Unrecyclable”
Zero Waste Church: Helping Churches Care for Creation

title: Waste Audit & Reduction
text:
Waste is often a sign of “system leakage”—resources bought and paid for that end up as environmental liabilities.

The Methane Problem: Organic waste in landfills produces methane, a greenhouse gas significantly more potent than CO2.
The Cost of Convenience: Single-use plastics and high-volume trash hauling are recurring expenses that can be redirected toward mission and ministry.

title: Waste Audit & Reduction
text:
Foundational steps to understand your current waste stream.



The 24-Hour Sort: Collect 24 hours’ worth of typical parish waste. With gloves and a tarp, sort the bags into three categories: True Trash, Recyclables, and Organics/Compostables.
Visual Baseline: Note the “contamination rate”—how much recyclable material is currently being thrown in the trash, and how much trash is in the recycling bins.
Contract Review: Examine your current waste hauling contract. Are you paying for “pulls” (pickups) of half-empty dumpsters? Adjust the frequency to match your actual needs.





Single-Use Audit: Identify the top 3 single-use plastic items used by the parish (e.g., water bottles, plastic forks, Styrofoam cups) and set a date to transition to reusable or compostable alternatives.

title: Waste Audit & Reduction
text:
Measures to improve efficiency and cultural habits.



The “Twin Bin” System: Ensure that every trash can on campus is paired with a clearly labeled recycling bin. Bins should be color-coded and feature icons (images of bottles/paper) rather than just text.
Bulk Purchasing: Transition from individual-sized items (milk carafes, sugar packets, bottled water) to bulk dispensers in the kitchen and parish hall.





Waste Education: Host a “Trash Talk” during coffee hour to show parishioners exactly what can and cannot be recycled in your specific municipality.
Signage Audit: Replace faded or confusing signs. Use photos of actual items used in the parish to eliminate guesswork.

title: Waste Audit & Reduction
text:
Advanced strategies for total waste diversion and neighborhood impact.



On-Site Composting: Establish a “Bokashi” or traditional compost bin for kitchen scraps. The resulting “black gold” can be used to fertilize the church’s Resilient Landscaping.
TerraCycle Hub: Become a community drop-off point for “hard-to-recycle” items not accepted by city bins (e.g., snack wrappers, alkaline batteries, electronics, or light bulbs).





Zero-Waste Events: Commit to making the Parish Picnic or Annual Meeting “Zero-Waste” by using only reusables or certified compostables and providing a “sorting station” staffed by youth volunteers.
Circular Economy Partnership: Partner with local schools or community gardens to provide compost or to source “ugly” produce that would otherwise be wasted.






EPA: How to Start a Waste Reduction Program
TerraCycle: Recycling the “Unrecyclable”
Zero Waste Church: Helping Churches Care for Creation

title: About
text:
The Creation Care and Sustainable Church Task Force of the Episcopal Diocese of Texas (EDOT) is a body of lay leaders commissioned to steward our physical and spiritual resources.
Launched in its current form in September 2025, we provide congregations with the technical tools and theological grounding necessary to meet the environmental challenges of our region.






Our Mission: Sacramental Stewardship



We believe that the Earth is a divine gift. Our care for it is a visible sign of an inward and spiritual grace. In the heart of “energy country,” we model radical transformation by treating carbon reduction and property resilience as essential acts of faith.








Reducing our environmental footprint to reach Net Zero by 2035–2040.



Hardening our facilities against natural disasters to serve as community “refugia.”



Breaking “climate silence” through theological education and liturgical practice.







Diocesan Audits: Set up a framework that will allow all congregations to do an environmental audit.
Financial Assistance: Developing strategies for congregations to get affordable upgrades of their utilities, including solar panel installations.


Back to Home

title: Toilets and Faucets
text:
Goal: To reduce indoor potable water usage, thereby decreasing parish expenses, lowering the energy required for water treatment, and extending the capacity of local water resources.


Reducing water use is a concrete step every parish can take toward environmental stewardship. Leaky or outdated fixtures can waste thousands of gallons of water per month. For example, a single leaking toilet can cost up to $50 per month in wasted water. These simple changeouts do not require professional water usage audits to begin making an impact.




Essential, low-cost steps with immediate financial and environmental benefits.



Leak Detection: Test all toilets for leaks at least twice per year and repair them immediately.
Flow Verification: Check the gallons per flush (gpf) on existing toilets; this is typically printed on the outside of the toilet, the inside of the tank, or the bottom side of the tank cover.






High-impact upgrades that significantly reduce ongoing resource consumption.



Faucet Aerators: Replace high-flow lavatory faucet aerators with 0.5 GPM or 1.0 GPM versions.

Note: Try 0.5 GPM aerators on a few faucets first to gauge effectiveness; if water pressure is insufficient, use 1.0 GPM aerators.

Pipe Insulation: Insulate all hot water lines—not just those in exterior walls—with R-3 or higher insulation to maintain water temperature and reduce waste.






Long-term investments for when equipment fails or major renovations occur.



WaterSense Toilets: If current toilets use more than 1.6 gpf, replace them with WaterSense labeled toilets or adjust flow rates to 1.28 gpf or lower.

Note: Ensure existing wastewater plumbing is sloped adequately to handle current federal maximum toilet flows.







Lower Utility Costs: Parish water bills will decrease significantly.
Resource Protection: Stress on local and regional water resources, including aquifers, rivers, and lakes, will be reduced.






EPA WaterSense Labeled Toilets: A guide to finding high-efficiency, high-performance models.

title: Cold & Freezing Resilience
text:
Goal: To prevent water damage from burst pipes, maintain facility warmth, and provide a safety net for vulnerable community members during extreme arctic events.


While average winter temperatures are rising, instabilities in the jet stream can cause occasional, extreme arctic cold to reach deep into Texas, as seen in the severe freeze of February 2021. Preparation must account for both facility protection and community refuge.




Essential steps to prevent structural damage and ensure basic safety.



Pipe Insulation: Ensure all exposed exterior and attic pipes are thoroughly insulated.
Water Utility Access: Identify the water shut-off meter and ensure the necessary tools are on-site and accessible to staff.





Heating System Audit: Perform professional annual maintenance on all heaters and HVAC units to ensure reliability before the first freeze.
Ice Storm Mitigation: Trim trees near buildings and power lines to prevent damage from falling limbs weighed down by ice.






Measures that improve energy efficiency and provide a secondary layer of defense.



Envelope Sealing: Weather-strip all doors and windows to prevent heat loss.
Thermal Insulation: Add insulation to walls or attics to keep the facility’s interior warmth stable during power outages.





Pipe Draining: If a severe freeze is imminent, turn off the water at the meter and drain the pipes entirely.
Internal Heating: Open kitchen and bathroom cabinet doors to allow warmer building air to circulate around internal pipes.
Travel Safety: Encourage staff and parishioners to stay home and off the roads until ice conditions improve.






Advanced plans for when the church serves as a regional refuge.



Warming Center Protocol: Develop a formal plan for hosting a community warming center, including staffing and supply logistics.
Vulnerable Housing: Establish a plan to house vulnerable parishioners—especially those who lose power or heat—by coordinating with families who have extra space or backup power.





Resource Matching: Maintain an evergreen list of parishioners who would need assistance getting to a warm place and those willing to provide transportation or temporary housing.

title: Water Audit Resources
text:
Goal: To connect congregations with local utility programs that provide free or subsidized water audits, equipment rebates, and infrastructure funding.



Several major Texas utilities offer specific programs to help non-profits and commercial customers identify waste and offset the cost of high-efficiency upgrades.


Houston offers comprehensive tools for its commercial and institutional partners to manage water demand.

Free Audit Tools: Access the Smart Utility Check Up and the Goldwater Dashboard Planning Tool to monitor and manage facility water use.
Direct Support: The water conservation team provides presentations and resources specifically for churches and organizations.
Contact: Email WaterConservation@houstontx.gov.




Austin provides one of the most robust financial incentive packages in the state for houses of worship and non-profits.

Water Efficiency Audit Rebate: Austin Water covers 75% of the cost of a professional water audit, up to a maximum of $5,000.
Requirement: The facility must be separately metered and use at least 100,000 gallons of water annually.
“Bucks for Business”: Incentive programs for large-scale equipment retrofits, such as cooling tower upgrades or process water reuse.
Free Tools: Free high-efficiency faucet aerators and showerheads are often available for qualifying multi-family or school-related facilities.




Fort Worth emphasizes education and direct hardware replacement through its “Smart” program suite.

SmartFlush Commercial: Congregations can receive vouchers for up to 10 free low-flow toilets (additional units may be requested after inspection).
Smart Irrigation Evaluation: Free professional evaluations of your irrigation system to identify leaks, broken heads, and inefficient controller settings.
Business Smart: A commercial-focused assessment program to reduce a facility’s overall water footprint.




Congregations in LCRA’s service area (including many in the Hill Country and Central Texas) have access to significant “WaterSmart” funding.

Audit Rebates: LCRA provides rebates of up to $125 per hour for professional indoor/outdoor water audits, with a cap of $5,000.
Project Funding: Receive a rebate for 50% of the cost (up to $20,000) for installing water-saving technologies like rainwater harvesting or cooling tower recycling.
Fixture Incentives: Standard rebates cover 50% of the cost for high-efficiency toilets ($100/unit), soil moisture sensors ($900), and smart controllers ($100).

Before applying for these programs, ensure you have 24 months of utility bills ready and have identified all water meters on your property. Most rebates require pre-approval before work begins.

title: Lightning & Surge Resilience
text:
Goal: To protect essential equipment—including sound systems, organs, and computers—from electrical spikes and to ensure the safety of all individuals on church grounds.



Lightning poses a high to very high risk throughout the diocese. Because of this significant threat, we have categorized protection measures into a clear hierarchy of implementation.




These measures are required for everyone to ensure life safety and core structural protection.



Lightning Protection Systems: Make certain that all buildings, especially those with high steeples, have professionally installed lightning rods (air terminals) that are properly grounded to the earth.
Safety Literacy: Ensure that all staff members are fully aware of lightning safety procedures.





Safety Review: Formally remind all staff of lightning safety procedures, with a specific focus on safety during outdoor activities and community events. Outdoor summer activities should always have a “plan B” in case lightning threatens.






These measures provide a significant layer of defense for the church’s digital and financial assets.



Point-of-Use Protection: Surge protectors are highly recommended for all computers, sound boards, projectors, servers, and other sensitive electronics. Use high-joule strips for maximum defense.
Whole-Building Surge Protection: Install a Type 1 or Type 2 surge protector at the main electrical panel to intercept high-voltage spikes before they enter the building’s internal circuits.
Data Stewardship: Utilize cloud-based backups for all church records. This ensures that a direct strike to an office computer does not result in the total loss of irreplaceable historical or financial data.






For enhanced situational awareness during outdoor services or regional events.



Detection Technology: Consider purchasing a handheld lightning detector to provide real-time alerts for incoming storms, allowing for earlier and safer evacuations of outdoor gatherings.





When installing lightning rods or whole-building surge protection, always use a licensed electrician or a certified lightning protection specialist to ensure the system meets modern safety codes.
